name: Acerola (Barbados Cherry)
description: Highest natural vitamin C content of any food — up to 80x more than oranges. Very popular in QLD. Bright red berries, slightly tart.
notes: Frost sensitive. Harvest when red and slightly soft. Doesn't keep well — eat fresh or freeze immediately.

name: Agapanthus
description: South African perennial forming dramatic clumps of strap-like leaves with tall stems of massed blue or white trumpet flowers in summer. Extremely tough and drought-tolerant.
notes: Declared invasive in parts of Victoria and other states — check local regulations before planting. Deadhead before seeds set. Divide clumps every 5-6 years. Excellent coastal plant.

name: Almond
description: The classic Mediterranean nut tree, well-suited to dry Australian summers. Breathtakingly beautiful in bloom in late winter before the leaves emerge — clouds of white-pink blossom. Almonds need a dry harvest period — humidity and rain during hull-split causes disease.
notes: Needs another almond variety nearby for cross-pollination (most varieties not self-fertile). Harvest when the hull splits open and peels back easily. Dry in the shell for weeks before cracking. Nonpareil, Carmel, and Mission are common varieties.

name: Alpine Strawberry
description: Small but intensely fragrant wild-type strawberry. Fruits almost continuously. No runners — stays compact. Tolerates more shade than standard strawberries.
notes: Start from seed or seedlings. Perfect for edging beds. Never stops producing in mild climates.

name: Amaranth
description: Ancient grain crop with edible leaves, stems and seed heads. Young leaves are used like spinach; mature seeds harvested as a gluten-free grain. Highly heat and drought tolerant.
notes: Harvest young leaves regularly. Let some plants run to seed for grain harvest — thresh when papery bracts dry. Ornamental varieties (red/purple) equally edible.

name: Apple
description: Most varieties need a pollination partner and winter chill hours. Not suited to tropical climates.
notes: Requires 600-1200 chill hours. Thin fruit after June drop for better size. Prune to open vase shape.

name: Apricot
description: Early-flowering stone fruit that can be hit by late frosts. Dry summers ideal — brown rot thrives in humidity. Prolific producer.
notes: Frost at flowering ruins the crop. Plant on a south-facing slope if frost is a risk. Prune after harvest. Excellent dried.

name: Aquilegia (Columbine)
description: Graceful perennial with distinctive spurred flowers in soft purples, pinks, yellows and bicolours that nod delicately in the breeze. Self-seeds and hybridises freely.
notes: Self-seeds prolifically — allow seedheads to develop for naturalising. Prefers partial shade. All parts mildly toxic if eaten in quantity.

name: Aronia (Chokeberry)
description: Extraordinarily high in antioxidants. Black berries are very astringent fresh but excellent for juice, wine, and jam. Beautiful autumn foliage.
notes: Very tough and low maintenance. Tolerates wet soils and some shade. Berries persist on the plant.

name: Asparagus
description: 20-30 year perennial. Requires 2 years before first harvest. Absolutely worth the wait. Plant once, harvest forever.
notes: Do not harvest first 2 years — let ferns develop to feed crowns. Feed heavily after harvest season. Weed competition is the main challenge.

name: Atemoya
description: Hybrid between sugar apple and cherimoya, producing heart-shaped green fruits with smooth custard-like white flesh of extraordinary sweetness. Known as the 'custard apple' in Australia.
notes: Requires hand pollination early morning for reliable crops — use a small brush to transfer pollen between flowers. African Pride is the most widely grown Australian variety.

name: Avocado
description: Frost sensitive but popular in warm Aussie climates. Type A and Type B varieties improve pollination.
notes: Extremely sensitive to waterlogging. Must have perfect drainage. Mulch heavily but keep away from trunk.
pollination: Plant Type A + Type B for best yield

name: Babaco
description: Ecuadorian relative of papaya producing pentagonal yellow fruits with fizzy, fragrant flesh tasting of strawberry, papaya and pineapple. Seedless and entirely edible including skin.
notes: Frost sensitive — protect below 2°C. Grows well in pots. Fruits ripen from base to tip over weeks. Skin edible and high in pectin — use in jams. Propagate from basal shoots.

name: Banana
description: Technically a giant herb. The fastest fruiting plant in the tropics. Fruits once then main stem is cut and a sucker takes over.
notes: Heavy feeder and drinker. Cut main trunk after harvest and select one strong sucker. Protect from wind.

name: Basil
description: Classic companion for tomatoes. Repels aphids and improves flavour.
notes: Pinch flowers to extend harvest. Hates cold and frost. Water at base.

name: Bay Laurel
description: Slow growing but essentially permanent. Single tree provides a lifetime of bay leaves. Can be pruned into a formal topiary.
notes: Slow to establish — buy a decent-sized plant. Very long lived. Harvest any time — fresh or dried.

name: Bean (Bush)
description: Compact and fast. No staking needed. Great for small gardens.
notes: Sow in succession every 3 weeks for continuous harvest.

name: Bean (Climbing)
description: Nitrogen fixer — excellent for improving bed fertility. Very productive.
notes: Don't fertilise with nitrogen. Pick pods young for tenderness.

name: Bee Balm
description: Vibrant North American wildflower with shaggy red, pink or purple blooms. Flowers, leaves and petals are all edible with a mild oregano-mint flavour.
notes: Leaves make a delicious herbal tea. Excellent for attracting bees, butterflies and hummingbirds. Divide clumps every 2-3 years.

name: Beetroot
description: Both roots and leaves are edible. Very easy to grow.
notes: Soak seeds overnight before sowing. Each seed cluster produces multiple seedlings.

name: Bitter Melon
description: Staple of Asian and Indian cooking. Extremely bitter — acquired taste. Very vigorous in tropical climates.
notes: Harvest while green for eating. Turns red/orange when overripe. Trellis required.

name: Black Currant
description: Intensely flavoured, massively high in vitamin C. Best cooked. Makes excellent jam, cordial, and wine. Needs genuine cold winters.
notes: Prune out 1/3 of oldest stems each year. Fruits best on 1-year wood. Needs more chill than red currant.

name: Blackberry (Thornless)
description: Thornless varieties strongly recommended for home gardens. IMPORTANT: Wild blackberry is a declared weed in most of Australia — only grow thornless cultivars.
notes: Tip-prune canes in summer for more fruit. Wild blackberry is a declared weed — only grow named cultivars.

name: Blueberry
description: Needs acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Beautiful autumn colour. Plant two varieties for best yields.
notes: Acidify soil with sulfur. Use azalea/camellia fertiliser. Mulch with pine bark. Net against birds.

name: Bok Choy
description: Fast growing Asian green. Great for stir fries. Doesn't like heat.
notes: Can be harvested whole or leaf by leaf. Watch for white cabbage moth caterpillars.

name: Borage
description: Brilliant blue star-shaped edible flowers. Cucumber flavour. Excellent bee attractor. Self-seeds freely — one plant becomes a colony.
notes: Star flowers are edible — use in salads, as garnish, or freeze in ice cubes. Excellent companion for most plants.

name: Bottlebrush
description: Quintessential Australian shrub with cylindrical flower spikes in brilliant red, pink or white that attract birds and bees year-round.
notes: Hardy and adaptable to most soils including wet. Prune immediately after flowering. Low phosphorus fertiliser only.

name: Boysenberry
description: Raspberry × blackberry × loganberry hybrid. Large, dark, intensely flavoured fruit. Very popular in NZ and southern Australia.
notes: Train along wires. Fruit is soft — eat fresh or freeze immediately. Prune out fruited canes after harvest.

name: Broad Bean
description: Hardy cool-season legume. Pinch out growing tips to deter black aphids.
notes: Plant in autumn for spring harvest. Great nitrogen fixer for bed rotation.

name: Broccoli
description: Harvest the main head before it flowers. Side shoots continue for weeks.
notes: Net against cabbage moth. Feed with nitrogen-rich fertiliser mid-growth.

name: Brussels Sprouts
description: One of the longest-season brassicas. Frost greatly improves flavour. Harvest from the bottom up.
notes: Stake firmly — tall and top heavy. Remove yellowing lower leaves. Snapping off the top growing tip in late summer encourages sprout development.

name: Bush Tomato (Desert Raisin)
description: Desert shrub producing small raisin-like fruits when dried. Key bush tucker ingredient. Intensely flavoured — used as a condiment or spice. Extremely drought tolerant.
notes: Very drought hardy once established. Fruits dry on the plant. Used in chutneys, sauces, and as a spice. Commercial seeds available.

name: Butternut Squash
description: Compact vines compared to most pumpkins. Sweet, nutty flesh. Stores well for months.
notes: Harvest when skin is hard and pale tan. Stem should be dry and corky. Store in cool dry place.

name: Cabbage
description: One of the oldest cultivated vegetables. Heads can be red, green, or savoy (crinkled). Stores well.
notes: Net against cabbage moth. Water consistently — irregular watering causes heads to split.

name: Calendula (Pot Marigold)
description: Orange and yellow edible flowers. NOT the same as Tagetes marigold. Petals used as a saffron substitute. Excellent medicinal herb for skin. Great companion plant.
notes: Self-seeds freely once established. Harvest flowers regularly to keep producing. Petals add colour to salads and rice.

name: Cape Gooseberry (Physalis)
description: Golden berries in papery husks. Unique sweet-tart tropical flavour. Self-seeds prolifically.
notes: Harvest when husk turns papery and brown. Don't eat green berries — mildly toxic when unripe.

name: Capsicum
description: Needs a long warm season. Can overwinter in frost-free climates.
notes: Mulch heavily to retain soil warmth. Pick green or leave to ripen red/yellow for sweetness.

name: Carambola (Starfruit)
description: Tropical Asian tree producing distinctive ribbed yellow fruits that create star-shaped slices. Sweet varieties have crisp, juicy flesh; sour types used in cooking and drinks.
notes: Frost sensitive. Fruits year-round in tropics. Choose named sweet varieties (Arkin, Kary) for fresh eating. Excellent ornamental with pink flowers. People with kidney disease should avoid.

name: Cardoon
description: Dramatic silver-grey architectural perennial — the wild ancestor of the artichoke. Blanched inner leaf ribs eaten braised or grilled in Mediterranean cooking. Stunning ornamental.
notes: Blanch stalks by wrapping with hessian or paper for 3-4 weeks before harvest to reduce bitterness. Can spread aggressively — declared a weed in SA; check local regulations.

name: Carrot
description: Deep, loose, rock-free soil is key. Sandy loam is ideal.
notes: Avoid fresh manure. Thin to 5cm. Sow direct, don't transplant.

name: Cassava (Tapioca)
description: Tropical staple producing large starchy tubers — the source of tapioca and a global food security crop. Extremely drought tolerant once established. Leaves also edible after cooking.
notes: IMPORTANT: Raw tubers and leaves contain cyanogenic compounds — must be thoroughly cooked. Sweet varieties are safest. Propagate from stem cuttings. Harvest before 18 months or tubers become woody.

name: Cauliflower
description: Fussier than broccoli. Needs blanching (tie leaves over curd) for white heads.
notes: Harvest when curd is firm and compact. Loosens quickly in heat.

name: Celeriac
description: The celery root. Knobby, ugly, absolutely delicious. Easier to grow than celery in cool climates. Stores well.
notes: Slow growing — start indoors 10-12 weeks before transplanting. Keep well watered. Mash or roast.

name: Celery
description: One of the more demanding vegetables but deeply rewarding. Needs consistently moist, rich soil and a long cool growing season. Blanching the stalks by wrapping or hilling makes them pale, tender, and milder in flavour.
notes: Never let it dry out — even brief drought causes stress and strong bitterness. Feed every 2 weeks with high-nitrogen fertiliser. Blanch stalks by tying them together for 2–3 weeks before harvest.

name: Chamomile (German)
description: Feathery, daisy-flowered annual. Flowers dried for tea. Attractant for beneficial insects. The 'physician of plants' — improves health of neighbouring plants.
notes: Self-seeds freely once established. Harvest flowers as they open fully. Dry immediately for best flavour.

name: Chamomile (Roman)
description: Low-growing perennial with apple-scented daisy flowers. Used for centuries to make soothing herbal tea. Can be used as a fragrant lawn substitute.
notes: Harvest flowers when fully open. Dry flowers for tea. 'Treneague' is a non-flowering lawn variety. Self-seeds freely. Tolerates light foot traffic.

name: Cherry
description: Needs the most chill hours of any stone fruit. Only suited to genuinely cold climates.
notes: Net against birds. Cover with plastic if rain forecast near harvest. Needs a pollinator in most cases.

name: Cherry Tomato
description: Prolific and easy to grow. More forgiving than large tomatoes. Great for beginners.
notes: Less prone to splitting than large varieties. Pick regularly to encourage production.

name: Chervil
description: The forgotten herb of French cuisine — delicate, anise-flavoured, and astonishingly underused outside France. Lacy leaves with a gentle flavour that disappears with cooking, so always add fresh at the end. One of the few herbs that thrives in shade.
notes: Direct sow in situ — does not like transplanting. Succession sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest. Bolts quickly in heat — strictly a cool-season herb. Never cook it — use only fresh.

name: Chickpea
description: Drought tolerant legume. Needs a long dry warm season. Very suitable for semi-arid Australian climates.
notes: Needs dry weather to mature. Harvest when pods are papery. Each pod contains 1-2 seeds.

name: Chilean Guava (Ugni)
description: Compact evergreen shrub producing tiny intensely fragrant strawberry-scented red berries — Queen Victoria's favourite fruit. Excellent hedging plant with pretty pink-white bell flowers.
notes: Harvest when dark crimson and very fragrant. Used to make the famous Chilean liqueur Murtado. Tolerates coastal conditions and light frost. Makes excellent informal hedge.

name: Chilli
description: The hotter the climate the hotter the chilli. Perennial in frost-free zones. Protect from frost and cold winds during cold months.
notes: Stress the plant slightly (reduce water once fruiting) to increase heat levels.

name: Chive Flowers
description: The purple pompom flowers of common chives are fully edible with a mild onion flavour. Excellent scattered over salads, soups and dips as a garnish.
notes: Both leaves and flowers edible. Deadhead to prevent excessive self-seeding. Divide clumps every 3 years. Companion plant that repels aphids and carrot fly.

name: Chives
description: Hardy perennial. Purple flowers are edible. Great pest deterrent.
notes: Cut back to base several times a year. Flowers edible in salads.

name: Choko
description: A sprawling vigorous vine producing pear-shaped, mild flavoured fruit. A staple in old Australian backyards — one vine can produce hundreds of fruit. Perennial in frost-free areas. Young shoots are also edible and taste like asparagus.
notes: Plant a whole fruit on its side, half-buried, with the stem end upward. Give it a very strong trellis or fence — vines can cover a large shed. Young shoots can be harvested and cooked like asparagus. Extremely productive.

name: Comfrey (Bocking 14)
description: Not eaten directly (slightly toxic) but one of the most valuable garden plants. Deep roots bring up minerals, leaves make the best fertiliser tea. Bocking 14 is a sterile hybrid that won't self-seed invasively.
notes: Cut leaves several times per year and use as mulch, compost activator, or steep in water for a powerful liquid fertiliser. Plant under fruit trees.

name: Coriander (Cilantro)
description: Bolts quickly in heat. Sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest.
notes: Let some plants bolt and self-seed. Seeds are also a spice (coriander seeds).

name: Corn (Sweet)
description: Wind pollinated — plant in blocks not rows. Classic Three Sisters companion with bean and pumpkin.
notes: Check ripeness by pressing a kernel — milky juice means ready. Eat same day as harvest for best sweetness.

name: Cosmos
description: Feathery-leaved annual with daisy-like flowers in pink, white and crimson. Extremely easy to grow from seed and self-seeds prolifically.
notes: Deadhead regularly to extend blooming. Tolerates poor soil — over-fertilising promotes foliage over flowers.

name: Cranberry
description: Very challenging in Australia — needs cool acid soil constantly moist. Better suited to Tasmania and alpine VIC/NSW.
notes: Needs pH 4.0-5.5 peaty soil. Easier in large pots with ericaceous compost.

name: Cucumber
description: Loves heat. Trellis to save space and improve airflow.
notes: Consistent watering prevents bitterness. Harvest frequently to keep plant producing.

name: Cumquat (Kumquat)
description: The hardiest citrus — tolerates light frost. Tiny whole-edible fruits. The skin is sweet; the flesh is sour. Beautiful ornamental tree. Excellent in pots.
notes: Eat whole — the sweet skin balances the sour flesh. Makes excellent marmalade. Most cold-tolerant of all citrus.

name: Curry Leaf
description: Aromatic South Asian tree essential to South Indian, Sri Lankan and Malaysian cooking. Fresh leaves have a distinctive curry fragrance entirely different from curry powder.
notes: Frost sensitive — grow in pot in cold climates and bring inside. Harvest young sprigs — leaves are best fresh (dried leaves lose much flavour). Remove suckers from base regularly.

name: Dahlia
description: Spectacular tuberous perennial producing dinner-plate to pompom flowers in an enormous colour range. Tubers can be lifted and stored over winter.
notes: Stake taller varieties. Pinch growing tips when 30 cm tall for bushy plants. Lift tubers after first frost in cold climates.

name: Daikon Radish
description: Giant white Japanese radish. Milder than small radishes. Also used as a green manure/soil breaker — the roots penetrate deep and break up compacted soil.
notes: Harvest when 30-40cm long. Also excellent left to bulk up into spring as a soil loosening tool.

name: Davidson's Plum
description: Rainforest tree producing intensely tart dark purple plums on the trunk. Quintessential bush tucker. Used in native cuisine for jam, sauce, and gin.
notes: Tolerates shade — ideal understory tree. Berries too sour raw but superb cooked. Supports biodiversity.

name: Delphinium
description: Majestic cottage perennial producing towering spikes of densely packed flowers in blues, purples, whites and pinks. Quintessential English cottage garden plant.
notes: Stake firmly — tall stems snap easily. Cut spikes back after first flush for a second bloom. All parts toxic if ingested. Prefers cool, moist conditions.

name: Dianthus / Carnation
description: Fragrant clove-scented flowers in vibrant reds, pinks, white and bicolours. Petals are edible and used to decorate cakes and crystallised as sweets.
notes: Petals edible — remove bitter white heel before eating. Excellent cut flower. Deadhead regularly. Prefers alkaline soil.

name: Dill
description: Annual herb with feathery leaves. Both leaves and seeds are used.
notes: Don't plant near fennel (cross-pollination) or carrot (stunts growth).

name: Echinacea / Coneflower
description: Hardy perennial native to North America with striking daisy-like flowers in pink, orange and white. Used medicinally as an immune booster and beloved by pollinators.
notes: Roots, leaves and flowers all used medicinally. Leave seed heads for birds in winter. Drought tolerant once established.

name: Edamame
description: Fresh soybeans harvested young — sweet, nutty, and enormously satisfying straight from the pod. The nitrogen-fixing legume you can eat. Steam or boil the whole pods and pop the beans into your mouth with a little salt.
notes: Harvest when pods are plump and bright green but before they start to yellow. The window is short — check daily. No fertiliser needed — fixes its own nitrogen.

name: Eggplant
description: Loves heat even more than tomatoes. Beautiful ornamental as well as edible.
notes: Pick before skin loses its gloss. Stake in windy positions.

name: Elderberry
description: Flowers and berries both edible when cooked. Flowers make elderflower cordial; berries make syrup, jam, and wine.
notes: IMPORTANT: Raw unripe berries are toxic — always cook before eating. Flowers are safe fresh. Birds love them.

name: Elderflower
description: Deciduous shrub producing large flat-topped clusters of tiny fragrant white flowers in early summer, followed by deep purple elderberries. Flowers used for cordials, fritters and champagne.
notes: Flowers best harvested in full bloom on dry days. Berries must be cooked before eating — raw are toxic. Elderflower cordial is a summer classic.

name: Endive / Witloof
description: Slightly bitter chicory-family leaf. Blanching (excluding light) reduces bitterness. Witloof is the Belgian endive forced in darkness for white chicons.
notes: Cover with black plastic or a bucket to blanch hearts white and reduce bitterness.

name: Feijoa
description: NZ favourite gaining popularity in southern Australia. Fragrant flowers with edible petals. Pineapple-guava flavour.
notes: Fruit falls when ripe. Plant two for better pollination. Very frost hardy. Can be grown as a hedge.

name: Fenugreek
description: Ancient legume herb grown for its bitter, maple-scented seeds used in Indian, Ethiopian and Middle Eastern cooking. Young leaves used as a vegetable (methi) in Indian cuisine.
notes: Sow direct — dislikes transplanting. Young leaves (methi) harvested from 3 weeks. Seeds harvested when pods yellow. Used medicinally to support blood sugar management.

name: Fig
description: One of the easiest and most rewarding backyard fruit trees. Self-fertile. Two crops per year possible.
notes: Do NOT fertilise with nitrogen. Prune in winter. Fruit drops if overwatered in summer.

name: Finger Lime
description: Australian native citrus with caviar-like pearls inside. Extremely trendy in high-end cuisine. Diverse colours — green, red, black, yellow. Thorny rainforest shrub.
notes: Very slow growing but increasingly available as grafted plants. Excellent hedge or screen. Drought tolerant once established.

name: Florence Fennel
description: A bulb-forming vegetable (distinct from herb fennel) with crisp, anise-flavoured bulbs. Both the bulb and fronds are edible. Notoriously allelopathic — secretes chemicals that inhibit nearby plants. Give it its own spot or container.
notes: Earth up around the swelling bulb to blanch it and keep it white and tender. Harvest when the bulb is golf-ball to tennis-ball size. Bolts quickly in heat — strictly a cool-season crop.

name: Forget-Me-Not
description: Classic spring biennial carpeting the ground with tiny sky-blue, pink or white flowers with yellow centres. Traditional partner to tulips and spring bulbs.
notes: Self-seeds prolifically — allow plants to set seed then remove for a colony next year. Tolerates shade. Short-lived but naturalises easily.

name: Foxglove
description: Stately biennial with towering spikes of tubular blooms in purples, pinks, white and cream. A magnet for bees and hummingbirds.
notes: All parts toxic if ingested — handle with care. Self-seeds freely for perennial display. Thrives in dappled shade.

name: French Tarragon
description: Essential French cooking herb. Strong anise flavour. Must be grown from cuttings — seed-grown plants are Russian tarragon (inferior flavour).
notes: Always buy French tarragon as a plant, not seeds. Dies back in winter, regrows from roots. Divide every 3 years.

name: Galangal
description: Aromatic Southeast Asian rhizome resembling ginger but with a pinier, more complex flavour. Essential in Thai tom kha gai soup, Balinese and Indonesian spice pastes.
notes: Grow like ginger — warm, moist, partially shaded. Young rhizomes are pink and milder; mature ones stronger and woodier. Dried and powdered as laos powder. Harvest in winter when dormant.

name: Garlic
description: Plant individual cloves in autumn for summer harvest. Improves pest resistance of nearby plants.
notes: Stop watering when lower leaves yellow. Hang in bunches to cure 3-4 weeks.

name: Ginger
description: Tropical rhizome. Grown from fresh supermarket ginger. Partial shade preferred. Beautiful ornamental foliage.
notes: Plant fresh root sections with buds. Dig when leaves yellow in autumn. Keep some rhizome to replant next season.

name: Globe Artichoke
description: Large, dramatic perennial. The flower bud is the edible part. Harvest before the bud opens. Stunning structural plant in the garden.
notes: Cut back hard after main crop. Divide every 3-4 years. Young offsets give quickest crop.

name: Globe Artichoke
description: Majestic Mediterranean perennial grown for its large, edible flower buds. Striking silver-grey architectural foliage makes it equally valued as an ornamental.
notes: Cut buds before they begin to open. Side-shoot buds are smaller but just as tender. Cut plant back hard after harvest. Divide clumps every 3-4 years.

name: Goji Berry
description: Extremely tough and drought tolerant. Bright red elongated berries. Fresh berries are milder than dried.
notes: Very adaptable to poor soils. Can become weedy — roots spread. Do NOT eat leaves.

name: Good King Henry
description: Ancient European perennial vegetable — once a common cottage garden food plant, now largely forgotten. Young shoots eaten like asparagus; leaves like spinach; seeds used like quinoa.
notes: Tolerates partial shade well. Harvest young shoots in spring for best flavour — use like asparagus. Leaves contain oxalic acid — eat in moderation. Spreads slowly by seed.

name: Gooseberry
description: Incredibly productive once established. Tolerates shade well. Spiny stems make it a good barrier plant.
notes: Thin fruits in early summer for larger berries. Wear gloves when pruning. Powdery mildew in humid climates.

name: Grape (Table)
description: Deciduous vine needing sturdy pergola or trellis. Long productive life — 30+ years. Table grapes grown for eating.
notes: Prune hard every winter — remove 90% of growth. Feed in spring only. Powdery mildew: spray sulfur preventatively.

name: Grape (Wine)
description: Wine grapes are smaller, sweeter and more complex than table grapes. Grown for home winemaking.
notes: Deliberately stress with low water and poor soil for more flavour. Same pruning as table grape.

name: Grapefruit
description: Largest of the common citrus. Needs long hot summers to ripen and sweeten properly. Ruby Red is the most popular variety.
notes: NOTE: Grapefruit interacts with many medications — check if relevant. Needs more heat than other citrus to develop sweetness.

name: Grevillea
description: Diverse Australian genus of over 360 species producing spider-like or toothbrush flower clusters beloved by honeyeaters and lorikeets.
notes: Never use phosphorus fertilisers — toxic to proteaceous plants. Prune lightly after flowering to maintain shape. Drought hardy once established.

name: Gypsophila (Baby's Breath)
description: Cloud-like masses of tiny white or pale pink flowers on wiry stems. An indispensable filler for cut flower arrangements and romantic cottage bouquets.
notes: Prefers alkaline soil — add lime to acidic soils. Annual types bloom quickly from seed. Cut when flowers are half open for longest vase life.

name: Heritage Rose
description: Old garden roses with full cupped blooms in blush, deep pink, crimson and purple, often with intense fragrance. The heart of the cottage garden.
notes: Feed with rose fertiliser from spring. Prune by one-third in late winter. Deadhead regularly. Watch for aphids — companion planting with lavender and chives helps.

name: Hollyhock
description: Towering cottage garden staple with large saucer-shaped flowers climbing the stem in pinks, reds, purples and white. Edible flowers and leaves.
notes: Flowers and young leaves are edible — use in salads or as a colourful garnish. Stake in exposed positions. Self-seeds prolifically.

name: Honeyberry (Haskap)
description: Japanese edible honeysuckle. One of the earliest fruiting plants of the year. Very frost hardy — flowers survive -7C.
notes: MUST have two compatible varieties — won't fruit alone. Excellent for Tasmania and alpine areas.

name: Horseradish
description: Fiercely pungent root with a punishing bite — the chemical burn that clears your sinuses instantly. Easy to grow but difficult to contain once established, as any piece of root left in the soil will regrow. Freshly grated root is incomparably hot and fresh compared to shop-bought.
notes: CONTAIN IT — plant in a bottomless pot buried in the ground. Harvest roots in autumn after first frost. The heat fades quickly once grated — use immediately or mix with vinegar to preserve it.

name: Jaboticaba
description: Unique Brazilian fruit tree that produces berries directly on the trunk. Bizarre and beautiful. Ferments quickly — eat within a day of picking.
notes: Slow growing but worth the wait. Multiple crops per year in warm climates. Makes extraordinary wine and jam.

name: Jerusalem Artichoke
description: Perennial sunflower relative grown for edible tubers. Extremely easy — almost too easy. Can be invasive if not contained. Tall sunflower-like plant.
notes: WARNING: Can spread extensively. Grow in a contained bed. Tubers cause flatulence in some people — start with small amounts.

name: Jostaberry
description: Black currant × gooseberry hybrid. Large dark berries with blackcurrant flavour but easier growing. Disease resistant. Thornless.
notes: Very vigorous — give it space. Prune to maintain open airy bush.

name: Jujube (Chinese Date)
description: Ancient Chinese fruit tree producing wrinkled red-brown fruits with apple-crisp flesh fresh, or chewy date-like texture when dried. Extremely drought and heat tolerant once established.
notes: One of the most heat-adapted deciduous fruit trees for inland Australia. Ripens late summer/autumn. Li and Lang are reliable varieties. Self-fertile. Thorny — handle with care.

name: Kakadu Plum (Gubinge)
description: Northern Australian tree producing small tart green fruits with the highest recorded vitamin C content of any food — up to 100 times that of oranges. Now commercially cultivated for health products.
notes: Tropical savanna species requiring a dry season. Too tart to eat raw — used in jams, sauces and nutraceuticals. Requires dry season to perform well. Highly significant culturally for Aboriginal communities.

name: Kale
description: Superfood and one of the hardiest vegetables. Frost sweetens the flavour.
notes: Pick outer leaves first. Can harvest over many months. Cavolo nero is a popular Italian variety.

name: Kangaroo Paw
description: Iconic Western Australian native producing velvety tubular flowers shaped like a kangaroo's paw, in red, orange, yellow and pink. Exceptional cut flower.
notes: Cut spent flower stems to the base to encourage re-flowering. Divide clumps every 3 years. Avoid phosphorus fertilisers.

name: Kangkong (Water Spinach)
description: Tropical aquatic leafy vegetable staple across Southeast Asia. Hollow stems and tender leaves used in stir-fries, soups and as morning glory greens. Fast growing in warm, moist conditions.
notes: Declared a noxious weed in QLD and WA — check local regulations before growing. Grows in soil or shallow water. Harvest young shoot tips, new growth appears within days.

name: Kiwi Berry
description: Miniature, grape-sized kiwifruit with edible smooth skin — no peeling required. Sweet, intensely flavoured, and beautiful in fruit bowls and desserts. More cold-hardy than full-sized kiwifruit. Vigorous climbing vine that needs strong support.
notes: Needs male and female plants — one male pollinates several females. Fruit is ripe when it yields slightly to pressure and tastes intensely sweet. Can be eaten skin and all. More manageable than standard kiwifruit.

name: Kiwifruit
description: A vigorous deciduous climbing vine that can cover enormous structures — a mature plant can produce 50-100kg of fruit per season. Requires both a male and female plant (one male pollinates up to 8 female vines). Fruit should be stored at room temperature after harvest to ripen fully.
notes: Must have one male plant per 3-8 female vines. Train on a very strong pergola or trellis — mature vines are enormously heavy. Prune hard each winter to manage size and keep productive. Fruit is hard when harvested — store for weeks to soften.
pollination: Needs male and female plants — 1 male per 3-8 females

name: Kohlrabi
description: Swollen stem rather than a root. Mild broccoli-stem flavour. Purple or white varieties. Underused vegetable.
notes: Harvest at tennis ball size — larger becomes woody. Eat raw or cooked. Leaves also edible like kale.

name: Lavender
description: Highly aromatic Mediterranean perennial. Powerful bee and beneficial insect attractor. Edible flowers (sparingly — strong). Excellent pest deterrent.
notes: Cut back by half after flowering but never into old wood. Well-drained alkaline soil. English lavender is hardiest.

name: Leek
description: Milder flavour than onion. Frost hardy. Blanch by mounding soil around stems.
notes: Start in seed trays and transplant when pencil-thick. Leave in ground until needed.

name: Lemon
description: Most popular backyard citrus in Australia. Eureka and Lisbon fruit almost year-round. Dwarf rootstocks for pots.
notes: Feed with citrus fertiliser in late winter, late spring, early autumn. Yellow leaves = nitrogen or iron deficiency.

name: Lemon Aspen
description: Queensland rainforest tree producing clusters of small pale yellow translucent fruits with intense lemon-eucalyptus flavour. A prized native citrus alternative used in dressings, desserts and cocktails.
notes: Requires tropical or subtropical climate with no frost. Self-fertile. Fruit used raw or as a flavouring — very tart. Suited to container growing. Becoming popular in restaurants.

name: Lemon Balm
description: Lemon-scented mint relative. Very easy to grow. Used for tea, cooking, and calming herbal remedies. Strong bee attractor.
notes: Can spread like mint — contain if needed. Cut back hard after flowering. Dries well for winter tea.

name: Lemon Myrtle
description: Most lemon-scented of all plants — even stronger than lemon itself. Leaves used fresh or dried in cooking, teas, and skincare. QLD rainforest tree now widely cultivated.
notes: Harvest leaves any time. Dry and store for cooking. Excellent in seafood, chicken, and desserts. Native bees love the flowers.

name: Lemon Verbena
description: A tall, woody perennial shrub with narrow leaves that pack an intensely clean lemon scent — more potent than lemongrass and more complex than lemon zest. The scent releases at the slightest touch. Exceptional in teas, desserts, and syrups. Deciduous in cool climates.
notes: Loses its leaves in winter but regrows vigorously each spring. Prune hard after summer to keep compact. Dry leaves retain their scent for months.

name: Lemongrass
description: Tropical grass used extensively in SE Asian cooking. Also makes a beautiful ornamental clump. Very frost sensitive.
notes: Bring inside or heavily mulch in winter in cool climates. Divide large clumps every 2-3 years. Harvest outer stalks.

name: Lettuce
description: Cool season crop. Bolts in heat. Partial shade in summer extends season.
notes: Cut-and-come-again varieties give multiple harvests. Bitter when bolting.

name: Lilly Pilly
description: Popular native used as hedging that also produces edible berries. Pink/red/purple berries taste like spiced apple or rosewater. Dozens of species.
notes: Berries vary in flavour by species. Used for jam, chutney, and gin. Psyllid insect causes warty foliage on some varieties.

name: Lime (Tahitian)
description: Most common variety in Australia. Seedless and juicy. More cold sensitive than lemons.
notes: Protect from frost. Fruits when green — don't wait for yellow.

name: Lingonberry
description: Scandinavian cranberry relative. More adaptable than cranberry but needs acid soil and cool climate. Two crops per year. Evergreen groundcover.
notes: Similar growing conditions to blueberry. Excellent groundcover under acid-loving plants. Berries very tart — best cooked.

name: Loganberry
description: Raspberry × blackberry cross. Long dark red berries with strong tart flavour. Excellent for jam and cooking.
notes: Needs a sturdy trellis. More vigorous than raspberry. Prune similarly to blackberry.

name: Loquat
description: Underrated. Fruits in late winter/spring when little else is available. Extremely hardy once established.
notes: Very drought-tolerant once established. Minimal care needed. Thin fruit clusters for larger fruit.

name: Lovage
description: Giant celery-flavoured perennial herb — all parts edible: leaves, stems, seeds and roots. One plant provides a season's worth of celery flavouring for soups, stews and salads.
notes: Cut back hard in midsummer to regenerate fresh young leaves. Leaves strongest in spring. Seeds used as a spice. Excellent wildlife plant — bees love the flowers.

name: Lupin (Edible)
description: Edible lupin varieties (not ornamental) produce protein-rich seeds. Also an excellent nitrogen-fixing green manure crop.
notes: Ensure you have edible variety — ornamental lupins are bitter/mildly toxic. Often grown as green manure and dug in before seeding.

name: Lychee
description: A slow-growing evergreen tree that produces clusters of intensely aromatic, translucent white-fleshed fruit inside rough pink-red skin. Patience is essential — trees take years to fruit but then reward abundantly for decades. Cold stress in winter (not frost) actually triggers better flowering.
notes: Needs a period of cool dry weather (10-15C nights) in winter to stimulate flowering — no actual frost. Acid soil preferred (pH 5.0-5.5). Kwai Mai Pink, Salathiel, and Sweetheart are popular Australian varieties.

name: Macadamia
description: Australia's famous native nut tree, now grown worldwide. One of the world's most prized nuts — buttery, rich, with a distinctive crunch. Slow to establish but incredibly long-lived; trees can produce for over 100 years.
notes: Use low-phosphorus fertiliser — like all Proteaceae, it's sensitive to high phosphorus and will die. Harvest nuts as they drop naturally. Plant at least two varieties for cross-pollination. A4, 344, and Beaumont are popular varieties.

name: Makrut Lime (Kaffir Lime)
description: Southeast Asian citrus grown primarily for its distinctive double-lobed leaves used in Thai and Indonesian cooking. Leaves add unmistakeable floral citrus fragrance to curries, soups and stir-fries.
notes: Frost sensitive. The rough-skinned fruit is extremely sour but zest is used in cooking and the juice in hair products. Harvest leaves year-round. Grows well in pots.

name: Malabar Spinach
description: Tropical climbing perennial producing thick, succulent, mild-flavoured leaves. The summer substitute for regular spinach in hot climates — thrives when true spinach bolts.
notes: Needs trellis or fence. Red-stemmed variety is ornamental. Harvest young shoot tips and leaves. Mucilaginous when cooked — best raw in salads or added at the last minute to stir-fries.

name: Mandarin
description: Easier to peel than oranges. Imperial and Encore are popular. Good in pots on dwarfing rootstock.
notes: Alternate bearing is common. Thin fruit after a big flush.

name: Mango
description: Quintessential tropical fruit. Needs a dry winter to flower, warm wet summer. Not for cool or temperate climates.
notes: Reduce water in winter to trigger flowering. Grafted trees fruit in year 3-4. Anthracnose is the main disease.

name: Marigold (Tagetes)
description: Powerful pest deterrent. Roots repel nematodes. Flowers deter aphids and whitefly. Petals edible as garnish. The single most useful companion plant for the vegetable garden.
notes: Plant thickly around vegetable beds. Deadhead to extend flowering. French marigolds (small) are better pest deterrents than African (large).

name: Marjoram
description: The sweeter, gentler cousin of oregano. Marjoram has a more delicate, floral flavour where oregano is robust and peppery. An essential herb in Mediterranean, Middle Eastern, and European cooking. Wonderful with lamb, eggs, mushrooms, and in stuffings.
notes: Harvest before flowering for best flavour. Dries beautifully — hang bunches upside down in a warm, airy spot. More tender than oregano and less frost-hardy; treat as annual in cold climates.

name: Medlar
description: Ancient European fruit tree producing russet-brown, crown-shaped fruits that must be 'bletted' (frosted and slightly rotted) before eating. Complex spiced apple-caramel flavour used in jelly and cheese.
notes: Harvest after first frost. Leave fruits in a cool place until flesh softens ('bletting') — 2–4 weeks. Largely self-fertile. Outstanding ornamental with gnarled form and autumn colour.

name: Mint
description: Very vigorous — grow in a pot to contain spreading.
notes: Cut back hard after flowering. Tolerates shade. Repels pests.

name: Mizuna
description: Feathery Japanese green with mild peppery flavour. Very versatile in salads or stir fries. Bolt resistant compared to other Asian greens.
notes: Cut-and-come-again. Can harvest multiple times. Red mizuna is beautiful and identical in use.

name: Mountain Pepper (Tasmanian Pepper)
description: Cool-temperate Australian native with intensely hot, spicy leaves and berries used as a native spice substitute. Leaves and berries dried and ground as 'mountain pepper' in native cuisine.
notes: Both leaves and berries intensely hot — use sparingly. Prefers cool, moist, partially shaded conditions. Very ornamental with deep green aromatic foliage. Separate male/female plants needed for berries.

name: Mulberry
description: One of the best and most underrated backyard fruit trees. Enormous crops, minimal care.
notes: WARNING: Berries stain everything permanently. Plant away from paths and washing lines. Harvest daily or spread a sheet underneath.

name: Muntries (Emu Apple)
description: SA native groundcover producing spiced apple-flavoured berries. One of the most important Aboriginal food plants of southern Australia. Very drought tolerant.
notes: Incredibly drought hardy. Used in sauces, chocolate, and gin. Needs excellent drainage.

name: Mustard Greens
description: Pungent, peppery Asian and Southern US cooking staple. Young leaves are milder. Very fast growing cool-season crop.
notes: Flavour intensifies in cold weather. Young leaves good raw; mature leaves better cooked. Red varieties are ornamental.

name: Nashi Pear
description: Asian pear producing large, round, crisp and juicy fruits with a grainy-sweet texture. Unlike European pears, nashis ripen on the tree and stay crisp for weeks after picking.
notes: Requires a pollination partner (another nashi or compatible European pear). Thin to one fruit per spur for large fruits. Store at room temperature up to 4 weeks.

name: Nasturtium
description: Entire plant is edible — flowers, leaves, and seeds. Peppery rocket-like flavour. Excellent trap crop for aphids.
notes: Don't over fertilise — produces lush leaves at expense of flowers. Seeds can be pickled like capers.

name: Native Ginger (Alpinia)
description: Australian native ginger with large ornamental leaves and beautiful pink-white flowers followed by seed capsules. Seeds used as a native spice with a cardamom-pepper flavour.
notes: Seeds from red capsules are the main edible product — flavour is milder than true ginger. Stunning ornamental for shaded gardens. Spreading habit makes excellent ground cover.

name: Native Violet
description: Delicate Australian native ground cover with small purple and white flowers. Spreads by runners to form a dense weed-suppressing mat.
notes: Excellent lawn substitute in shaded areas. Moderately frost tolerant. Edible flowers can garnish salads.

name: Nectarine
description: A smooth-skinned genetic variant of peach — not a peach-plum hybrid as commonly believed. Sweetly aromatic with a firm, snappy texture when ripe. Often considered more flavourful than peaches. More susceptible to brown rot and leaf curl than peaches — good airflow is essential.
notes: Prune to an open vase shape for good air circulation to minimise fungal disease. Apply copper spray in late winter before bud swell to prevent leaf curl. Net fruit as it ripens — birds are devastating.

name: Nigella (Black Seed)
description: Annual with finely divided foliage and pale blue flowers followed by inflated seed pods. Seeds (nigella/black seed/kalonji) used as a spice in Middle Eastern, Indian and Turkish breads and curries.
notes: Self-seeds freely — allow some pods to burst and scatter seed for next year. Harvest pods when they rattle. Seeds have mild onion-oregano flavour. Also ornamental — dried seed heads excellent in arrangements.

name: Oca
description: Andean tuber producing colourful pink, red, yellow and white waxy tubers with a lemony-earthy flavour. Gaining popularity as a heritage food crop and potato alternative.
notes: Day-length sensitive — only forms tubers as days shorten (autumn). Leave in ground until after first light frost for best yields. Tubers sweeten in sunlight for a few days after harvest.

name: Okra
description: Loves heat and humidity. Popular in African, Indian, and Southern US cooking. Beautiful hibiscus-like flowers.
notes: Harvest pods when 5-10cm — tough and fibrous if left longer. Wear gloves — plant is spiny. Very productive in tropical climates.

name: Olive
description: Incredibly long-lived and drought tolerant. Both table and oil varieties available.
notes: Almost zero fertilising needed. Alternate bearing is common. Check local council rules — declared weed in some areas.

name: Onion
description: Start from seeds or seedlings. Day length triggers bulbing.
notes: Stop watering once tops fall over. Cure in sun for 2 weeks before storage.

name: Orange (Navel)
description: Navel oranges are seedless and easy to peel. Washington Navel is the classic Aussie variety. Ripens in winter.
notes: Needs warm summers to sweeten fruit. Don't prune heavily. Thin fruit in heavy years for better size.

name: Oregano
description: Mediterranean perennial. Flavour is strongest in warm, dry, sunny conditions. Greek oregano is strongest flavoured.
notes: Cut back hard after flowering. Better flavour in poor soil — too much fertiliser makes it rank.

name: Pak Choy
description: Fast-growing Asian brassica with tender mild leaves and crisp juicy stems. Baby pak choy is harvested at 10–15 cm, full heads at 25–30 cm. Staple of stir-fries and Asian cooking.
notes: Bolt-resistant varieties best for spring/summer. Consistent watering prevents premature bolting. Entire plant edible from seedling stage. Beware slugs on young plants.

name: Pandan
description: Tropical Southeast Asian plant with long strap-like leaves with distinctive sweet vanilla-coconut-like flavour. Essential in Indonesian, Thai and Malaysian desserts, rice and chicken dishes.
notes: Frost sensitive — pot culture works well in cooler climates. Harvest leaves as needed. Blend leaves with water to extract green food colouring and flavour. Propagate from suckers.

name: Pansy (Edible)
description: Large-flowered viola grown specifically for edible use. Blooms in a rich palette of velvety purples, yellows, oranges and bicolours. Perfect for decorating cakes, cocktails and salads.
notes: Flowers have mild, slightly sweet flavour. Harvest in the morning when fresh. Cool-season annual — plant in autumn/winter in warm climates.

name: Parsley
description: Biennial. Slow to germinate (3-4 weeks). Very productive once established.
notes: Soak seeds 24h before sowing. Harvest outer stems. Goes to seed in year 2.

name: Parsnip
description: Slow to germinate (3-4 weeks) but rewarding. Frost and cold greatly improve sweetness. Related to carrot but sweeter when cooked.
notes: Needs fresh seed each season — old seed has poor germination. Sow thickly and thin. Frost converts starch to sugar.

name: Passionfruit
description: Fast-growing vine, productive from year 1. Needs a sturdy trellis. Short-lived (5-7 years) but easily replaced.
notes: Feed in spring and summer. Replace every 5-7 years. Susceptible to woodiness virus — buy certified virus-free plants.

name: Pawpaw / Papaya
description: A fast-growing tropical tree that can fruit within 12 months of planting. The large, sweet orange-fleshed fruit is one of the most nutritious tropical fruits. Grows extremely fast and produces prolifically in frost-free climates. Red-fleshed varieties have superior flavour.
notes: Frost sensitive — protect young plants. Plant multiple seedlings as some will be male. Red varieties (Sunrise, Red Lady) are sweeter than yellow. The milky white latex from green fruit contains papain — a meat tenderiser. Feed heavily with nitrogen-rich fertiliser.

name: Pea (Snow/Snap)
description: Cool season nitrogen fixer. Eat pods whole or shell them.
notes: Trellis for climbing varieties. Pick daily once fruiting to maximise yield.

name: Peach / Nectarine
description: One of the fastest stone fruits to bear. Self-fertile. Needs hard annual pruning.
notes: Prune by up to 50% each winter. Thin fruit heavily for large juicy peaches. Leaf curl: spray copper in winter.

name: Peanut
description: Warm-season legume that flowers above ground then bends its stems down to push developing pods into the soil. Excellent nitrogen fixer for tropical and subtropical gardens.
notes: Plant in loose, sandy, well-drained soil so pegs can penetrate easily. Harvest when leaves yellow and skins of sample pods have pink/purple veining. Dry pods thoroughly before storing.

name: Pear
description: Slower to bear than apples but long-lived. Harvest before fully ripe and ripen indoors.
notes: Pick when firm and ripen at room temperature. Fire blight is the main disease issue.

name: Perilla (Shiso)
description: Japanese herb used in sushi, tempura, and salads. Green (shiso) and red/purple varieties. Anise-basil-mint flavour. Self-seeds prolifically.
notes: Both leaf colours edible. Red perilla used for colouring umeboshi plums. Let some go to seed for next year.

name: Persimmon
description: A stunning ornamental deciduous tree that produces deeply orange, glossy fruit in late autumn after the leaves drop. Two types: astringent (must be fully soft before eating) and non-astringent (eat crisp like an apple). Hachiya = astringent; Fuyu = non-astringent.
notes: Non-astringent varieties (Fuyu, Izu) are eaten crisp. Astringent varieties (Hachiya, Nightingale) must ripen until jelly-soft or they're mouth-puckeringly bitter. Leave fruit on the tree after leaf drop for a spectacular display. Extremely low maintenance once established.
pollination: Most varieties self-fertile; some benefit from cross-pollination

name: Plum
description: Japanese plums most popular in Australia. Some are self-fertile; others need a pollinator.
notes: Prune after harvest. Brown rot is the main issue — remove mummified fruit.

name: Pomegranate
description: Extremely drought tolerant and beautiful. Loves heat. Deciduous. Stunning orange flowers.
notes: Minimal fertilising needed. Fruit cracks if overwatered near ripening. Harvest when skin turns red/bronze.

name: Popcorn Corn
description: Grown same as sweet corn but left to fully dry on the stalk. Very rewarding to grow your own popcorn. Isolate from sweet corn to prevent cross-pollination.
notes: Leave cobs on plant until completely dry. Husk and dry further indoors. Test by popping a few kernels before harvesting all.

name: Potato
description: Grown from seed potatoes. Hilling soil up around stems dramatically increases yield.
notes: Stop watering when foliage yellows. Leave in ground 2 weeks after vines die for skin to set.

name: Pumpkin
description: Space hungry but rewarding. Classic 'three sisters' companion with corn and bean.
notes: Lift fruit off soil on a tile to prevent rot. Cure in sun for 2 weeks after harvest.

name: Quandong
description: Desert native producing bright red fruits highly prized in bush tucker cuisine — rich in vitamin C with a tangy apricot-like flavour. Semi-parasitic on nearby plant roots.
notes: Requires a host plant nearby (any species). Very drought tolerant once established. Fruit used in jams, pies and sauces.

name: Quince
description: Underrated and beautiful. Fragrant golden fruit that must be cooked (too hard/tart raw). Makes the finest jelly and paste. Low maintenance once established.
notes: Fruit turns golden and smells heavenly when ripe. Never eaten raw. Makes brilliant quince paste (membrillo) and jelly.

name: Radicchio
description: Italian chicory forming tight heads of deep red and white leaves with characteristic bitter flavour that mellows when grilled or roasted. Beautiful in salads and on cheeseboards.
notes: Sow in late summer for autumn/winter harvest when cold weather intensifies red colour. Cut back scraggly summer plants to ground — compact red heads form in cool weather.

name: Radish
description: Fastest vegetable in the garden. Ready in as little as 3 weeks.
notes: Use as a row marker for slow-germinating crops. Goes woody if left too long.

name: Rainbow Chard
description: Identical to silverbeet in growing requirements but with stems in vivid red, orange, yellow, pink, and white. One of the most ornamental edible plants — often used as a border plant in flower gardens as well as the vegetable patch. Nutritious, productive, and stunning.
notes: A mix of colours in one planting is most striking. Stems are firmer than leaves — cook separately, starting stems first. Excellent in the ground or large pots. Very hardy and productive for months.

name: Raspberry
description: Cane fruit producing sweet-tart berries in summer. Primocane varieties fruit in autumn too. Needs support wires or trellis.
notes: After fruiting, cut out floricanes at ground level. Leave primocanes for next year's crop. Mulch heavily.

name: Red Currant
description: Strigs of jewel-like tart red berries. Tolerates partial shade. Excellent for jelly and cordial.
notes: Fruit on old wood — don't prune out spurs. Mulch heavily. Net against birds.

name: Rhubarb
description: Tart stalks used in desserts, jams, and drinks. LEAVES ARE TOXIC — only eat stalks. Long-lived perennial once established.
notes: IMPORTANT: Never eat the leaves — highly toxic (oxalic acid). Only red stalks are used. Needs cold winter dormancy to thrive.

name: Riberry
description: Subtropical rainforest tree producing clusters of small pear-shaped red fruits with a tart cranberry-clove flavour. Popular in native cuisine and gin botanicals.
notes: Tolerates shade and makes a good screen or hedge. Fruit best used in sauces, jams and desserts. Bird attracting.

name: Rocket (Arugula)
description: Peppery and fast growing. Goes bitter and bolts quickly in heat.
notes: Sow every 3 weeks for continuous harvest. Flowers are edible too.

name: Rockmelon (Cantaloupe)
description: Needs heat and dry air as fruit ripens. Very susceptible to powdery mildew.
notes: Ripe when stem detaches easily with gentle pressure and smells sweet. Reduce water near harvest.

name: Rose Hip (Rosa canina)
description: Dog rose grown for its fruit (hips). Extremely high in vitamin C. Hips used for tea, syrup, and jam. Ornamental flowering shrub.
notes: Harvest after first frost when hips turn deep red. Remove seeds and hairs before eating — the hairs cause irritation.

name: Roselle Hibiscus
description: Tropical edible hibiscus grown for its tart cranberry-flavoured calyces used in herbal teas, jams, syrups and salads. Beautiful ornamental too.
notes: Harvest calyces when swollen and fleshy, just after petals drop. Excellent for making hibiscus tea, cordial and dye. Needs warm frost-free climate.

name: Rosemary
description: Perennial Mediterranean herb. Drought tolerant once established. Excellent bee attractor.
notes: Don't overwater. Sandy well-drained soil. Prune after flowering to keep bushy.

name: Sage
description: Silvery-grey aromatic perennial. Repels cabbage moth. Strongly flavoured — use sparingly in cooking.
notes: Prune hard every spring to prevent woodiness. Good drainage essential. Purple and tricolour varieties are ornamental.

name: Saltbush (Old Man Saltbush)
description: Hardy Australian shrub with silver-grey leaves highly prized in bush tucker and fine dining. Salty, mineral-rich leaves used to season lamb and seafood. Exceptional drought and salt tolerance.
notes: Extremely drought, frost and salt tolerant. Used as stock fodder and landscape revegetation plant. Young leaves most tender. Female plants have seed capsules also used as seasoning.

name: Salvia (Ornamental)
description: Diverse genus of sages offering year-round flowers in electric blues, deep purples and scarlet reds. Exceptional bee, butterfly and hummingbird plant with long drought tolerance once established.
notes: Cut back by one-third after each flush of flowers. Very easy care once established. Hundreds of species suited to different climates — choose one matched to your zone. Avoid overwatering.

name: Scabiosa (Pincushion Flower)
description: Dainty perennial with domed pincushion flowers in soft lavender-blue, pale pink, white and deep burgundy. Outstanding long-stemmed cut flower beloved by bees and butterflies.
notes: Deadhead constantly to keep blooming. Perennial species more drought tolerant. Excellent for pollinators — plant in large drifts for impact.

name: Sea Kale
description: Unusual perennial sea vegetable from European coastlines. Blanched spring shoots have a delicate nutty asparagus-like flavour. Very long-lived and trouble-free once established.
notes: Force shoots in early spring by covering crown with pot or box to exclude light — produces sweet, mild blanched shoots. Also ornamental with large blue-green leaves and white flowers.

name: Serviceberry (Juneberry)
description: Multi-season interest: white spring flowers, sweet blueberry-like berries, brilliant autumn colour. Excellent for birds and wildlife.
notes: Can be grown as large shrub or small tree. Berries sweeter than blueberry with almond notes. Net against birds.

name: Shallot
description: More refined, sweeter flavour than onion. Each planted bulb multiplies into a cluster. Stores longer than regular onions.
notes: Plant individual bulbs like garlic. Harvest when tops dry off. Cure before storage.

name: Silverbeet (Swiss Chard)
description: More heat tolerant than spinach. A true workhorse of the Aussie veggie garden.
notes: Harvest outer leaves. Will crop for over a year if well fed. Rainbow chard is identical but colourful.

name: Snapdragon
description: Cool-season favourite with distinctive hinged blooms that snap open when squeezed. Outstanding cut flower with soft fragrance.
notes: Prefers cool weather — plant in autumn/winter in warm climates. Deadhead spent spikes to encourage side shoots.

name: Snow Pea
description: Flat-podded peas harvested before the peas inside develop — eaten pod and all. Sweet, crisp, and fresh. One of the best cool-season crops, producing quickly and prolifically. Children often eat them straight off the vine.
notes: Pick when pods are flat and still tender — if they start to plump, you have left them too long. Harvest daily at peak to keep plants producing. Nitrogen fixer — no fertiliser needed. Provide a trellis or netting.

name: Sorrel
description: A perennial leafy herb with a bright, lemony-sour flavour from its natural oxalic acid content. Among the first things to appear in spring and productive through most of the year. The French use it prolifically in soups, sauces with fish, and omelettes.
notes: Remove flower heads as they appear to maintain leaf production. Large older leaves are more sour — harvest young leaves for salads, mature leaves for cooking. High oxalic acid means it's best eaten in moderation.

name: Spinach
description: Bolts quickly in heat. Best grown in cool seasons or with shade cloth in summer.
notes: Silverbeet is a more heat-tolerant alternative for warm climates.

name: Spring Onion (Scallion)
description: Fast and easy allium. Harvest before bulbing for mild flavour. Sow every 3 weeks for continuity.
notes: Pull whole or cut leaves and they regrow. Great for container growing.

name: Statice (Sea Lavender)
description: Branching annual producing papery flowers in purple, pink, white, yellow and apricot that retain their colour when dried. An essential everlasting flower for dried arrangements.
notes: Harvest when 75% of florets are open. Hang upside down in a dry, airy place for 2-3 weeks. Extremely long-lasting dried. Tolerates coastal salt winds and alkaline soil.

name: Stevia
description: The natural sweetener plant. Stevia leaves contain steviol glycosides 200-300x sweeter than sugar with no calories. Grow your own guilt-free sweetener for teas and cooking. Harvest leaves before flowering when they're sweetest.
notes: Fresh leaves can be added directly to tea or infused into syrups. Dry and grind for a powdered sweetener. Pinch growing tips to keep bushy. Brings in beneficial insects when flowering.

name: Stock (Matthiola)
description: Intensely fragrant cottage flower with dense spikes of ruffled blooms in purples, pinks, red, white and cream. One of the most heavily scented of all cut flowers.
notes: Best fragrance in the evening. Prefers cool weather — sow in autumn/winter in warm climates. Excellent for cutting; scent fills a room. Dislikes transplanting.

name: Strawberry
description: Perennial. Remove runners unless propagating. Crop for 2-3 years before replacing.
notes: Mulch with straw to keep fruit clean. Net against birds. Renovate beds after harvest.

name: Sugar Snap Pea
description: The best of both worlds — round, plump pods with fully developed peas inside, but the whole pod is still crunchy and sweet. Eaten pod and all at peak ripeness. Exceptionally sweet — some consider them the best raw vegetable in the garden.
notes: Harvest when pods are plump and round but before they go leathery. A fresh pod makes a clean crisp snap. Best eaten within hours of picking when the sugars are highest. Needs staking.

name: Summer Savory
description: The bean herb. Strong, peppery flavour similar to thyme and marjoram. Traditional across Europe for flavouring bean dishes — it allegedly reduces the flatulence caused by beans when cooked together. Also excellent with grilled meats and in sausages.
notes: Harvest before flowering for strongest flavour. Sow directly where it's to grow — does not transplant well. Excellent companion for beans in the garden and on the plate.

name: Sunflower
description: Seeds edible roasted. Petals edible. Excellent at attracting beneficial insects and birds. Young buds can be eaten like artichokes. Tall varieties provide wind shelter.
notes: Direct sow after last frost. Birds will eat seeds — harvest heads when back turns yellow and hang to dry.

name: Swede (Rutabaga)
description: Hardy root vegetable — a cross between turnip and cabbage — with sweet, dense, creamy-yellow flesh. Improves in flavour after frost. Classic in soups, stews and roasts.
notes: Grows best in cool weather. Sow direct — dislikes transplanting. Harvest when 10–12 cm diameter; larger roots become woody. Stores well in the ground or cool cellar.

name: Sweet Alyssum
description: Low-growing annual covered in tiny honey-scented white or lilac flowers. Excellent edging plant and ground cover that attracts beneficial insects to the garden.
notes: Shear back by half in midsummer to encourage fresh flush of flowers. Excellent as a living mulch between vegetables. Self-seeds freely.

name: Sweet Pea
description: Sweetly scented climbing annual beloved for its delicate ruffled flowers in pastel and vibrant shades. A cottage garden icon.
notes: Soak seeds overnight before sowing. Provide trellis or fence. Pick flowers constantly — any pods forming stops flowering.

name: Sweet Potato
description: Tropical vine, very productive in warm climates. Leaves are also edible.
notes: Grown from slips (cuttings). Avoid nitrogen fertiliser. Cure at warm temperature for 2 weeks before eating.

name: Sweet Violet
description: Fragrant woodland perennial with deep purple, violet or white flowers. One of the oldest cultivated plants — flowers crystallised as confectionery since the Victorian era.
notes: Flowers and leaves edible. Crystallised violets used to decorate cakes. Spreads by runners. Thrives in dappled shade under trees.

name: Sweet William
description: Classic cottage biennial producing flat-topped clusters of fringed flowers in crimson, pink, white and striking bicolours. Lightly spicy-clove fragrance.
notes: Sow in late summer/autumn for flowers the following spring-summer. Cut flowers freely to extend the season. Self-seeds to persist in the garden.

name: Tamarind
description: Majestic tropical tree producing pods filled with sweet-sour brown paste used across Indian, Thai and Mexican cuisines. Extremely long-lived and drought tolerant.
notes: Very slow growing — years to first fruit but extremely long-lived (100+ years). Sweet Indian varieties preferred for fresh eating; sour types for cooking. Harvest pods when shells are brittle.

name: Taro
description: Tropical Pacific staple producing large elephant-ear leaves and starchy corms used in Pacific Island, Asian and Caribbean cooking. Extraordinarily productive in warm wet conditions.
notes: IMPORTANT: All parts contain calcium oxalate crystals — must be cooked before eating. Grow in consistently moist soil or shallow water. Harvest corms when outer leaves yellow.

name: Tatsoi
description: Rosette-forming Asian green with spoon-shaped leaves. Very cold hardy, even more so than bok choy.
notes: Can be harvested as baby leaves or as whole rosette. Tolerates light frost.

name: Tatsoi
description: Cold-hardy Asian green forming flat rosettes of dark spoon-shaped leaves with a mild mustard-spinach flavour. One of the fastest leafy greens from sow to harvest.
notes: Can be harvested as whole rosette or as cut-and-come-again leaves. Tolerates light frost. Sow successionally every 3 weeks for continuous harvest.

name: Tayberry
description: Blackberry × raspberry hybrid developed in Scotland. Larger than loganberry, sweeter flavour.
notes: Very vigorous grower. Spines are sharp on thorned variety. Pick when deep purple-red.

name: Thai Basil
description: More heat tolerant than sweet basil. Anise/liquorice flavour. Essential in Thai and Vietnamese cooking. Purple stems and flowers.
notes: More tolerant of heat and humidity than Italian basil. Flowers are edible and beautiful.

name: Thyme
description: Low-growing Mediterranean perennial. Incredibly drought tolerant once established. Bees love the flowers.
notes: Cut back by half after flowering. Well-drained soil essential. Many varieties — lemon thyme, creeping thyme.

name: Tomatillo
description: The essential ingredient in salsa verde. Small green-to-yellow fruits wrapped in a papery husk — ready when the husk splits open. Tart, citrusy flavour quite unlike tomatoes. Must grow at least two plants for cross-pollination.
notes: Needs two plants minimum for fruit set. Harvest when the husk splits and fruit fills it out. Remove the sticky coating by rinsing. Highly productive.

name: Tomato
description: One of the most popular home garden plants. Needs consistent moisture and full sun. Stake indeterminate varieties.
notes: Water deeply at base. Avoid wetting leaves. Feed potassium at flowering. Pinch laterals for larger fruit. Remove lower leaves to improve airflow. Remove any diseased leaves immediately. Pick when starting to turn colour and ripen indoors if you have pests or cracking issues.

name: Turmeric
description: Tropical rhizome related to ginger. Beautiful orange flesh when harvested. The anti-inflammatory spice staple. Grows similarly to ginger.
notes: Harvest when leaves die down in autumn/winter. Cure in warm conditions before using. Can be dried and ground.

name: Turnip
description: Fast-growing root vegetable. Both root and greens are edible. Young leaves excellent as salad greens.
notes: Harvest small (golf ball to tennis ball) for best flavour. Larger roots become woody and bitter.

name: Vietnamese Mint (Rau Ram)
description: Essential Southeast Asian herb despite the name — not related to true mint. Spicy, peppery with a coriander-like note. Loves wet conditions. Perennial in warm climates.
notes: Grows in or near water. Very easy to propagate from cuttings. Harvest stems regularly.

name: Viola / Pansy
description: Cheerful cool-season flower with fully edible blooms used to decorate salads, cakes and cocktails. Mild, slightly sweet flavour.
notes: Flowers are edible and make stunning garnishes. Prefers cool conditions — plant in autumn/winter in warm climates. Deadhead to prolong flowering.

name: Walking Onion (Tree Onion)
description: Unusual perennial onion that produces clusters of tiny bulbils on top of its stems. The weight bends stems over and the bulbils root where they touch soil — hence 'walking'. All parts edible year-round.
notes: Harvest bulbils from tops, green stems like shallots, or underground bulbs. Extremely hardy and self-perpetuating. Once planted, leaves the garden producing indefinitely with no replanting.

name: Wallflower (Erysimum)
description: Early-season cottage favourite producing dense clusters of richly scented flowers in warm golds, oranges, reds and purples. Traditional spring bedding plant.
notes: Sow in midsummer for flowering the following spring. Plant near seating areas to enjoy the sweet fragrance. Thrives in poor, well-drained soil.

name: Warrigal Greens (NZ Spinach)
description: Native groundcover eaten as spinach substitute. More heat tolerant than true spinach. Must be blanched before eating — reduces oxalic acid.
notes: IMPORTANT: Always blanch 30 seconds in boiling water and discard water before eating. Then use as regular spinach.

name: Watercress
description: Aquatic or semi-aquatic plant that thrives in constantly moist soil or shallow water. Peppery and highly nutritious.
notes: Keep soil constantly wet or grow in shallow water. Can grow in a container sitting in a tray of water. Harvest tips regularly.

name: Watermelon
description: Needs long hot summers. Reduce watering as fruit matures to concentrate sweetness.
notes: Thump test: ripe watermelon sounds hollow. Tendril near fruit stem goes dry when ripe.

name: Wattleseed (Acacia)
description: Edible seeds of various Acacia species, particularly A. victoriae and A. murrayana, are a prized bush tucker ingredient. Roasted seeds have a coffee-chocolate-hazelnut flavour.
notes: Avoid phosphorus fertilisers. Seeds roasted in pan before using to release flavour — use in breads, ice cream, coffee and damper. Nitrogen-fixer improves poor soils.

name: White Currant
description: Albino mutation of red currant. Sweeter and milder. Translucent beautiful berries. Less common but easy to grow.
notes: Grow exactly as red currant. Can be eaten fresh. Stunning in fruit salads.

name: White Sapote
description: Mexican tree producing round green or yellow fruits with soft, creamy white flesh with a sweet custard-vanilla-peach flavour. Sometimes called 'Mexican apple'. Highly productive.
notes: Fruit ripens off the tree — harvest when slightly soft and ripen indoors. Over-ripe fruit becomes narcotic-bitter. Suebelle and Vernon are reliable varieties in Australia.

name: Winged Bean
description: Remarkable tropical legume where every part is edible — pods, seeds, leaves, flowers and protein-rich tubers. Pods have distinctive frilled wings. Nutritionally one of the most complete vegetables.
notes: Needs trellis. Young pods best at 15 cm before wings harden. Tubers harvested at end of season. Day-length sensitive — flowers when days shorten. Perennial in tropics.

name: Witlof (Belgian Endive)
description: Produces compact, pale, bitter-sweet chicory heads (chicons) through a two-stage process: grow the root outdoors, then force the root indoors in the dark for 3-4 weeks to produce the pale, tightly folded chicons. Sophisticated and worth the effort.
notes: After a hard frost, dig up roots, trim tops to 2cm, pack upright in damp sand in a dark cool spot (10-15C). Chicons sprout in 3-4 weeks. Slightly bitter flavour is prized in French and Belgian cuisine.

name: Wombok (Chinese Cabbage)
description: The cabbage used in kimchi and Asian cooking. Mild, crisp flavour. Cool season only.
notes: Bolts quickly in heat. Net against cabbage moth. Harvest whole head.

name: Yacon
description: South American root with sweet, crisp, watery flesh — like a cross between apple and jicama. Low GI despite tasting sweet. Very underused in Australia.
notes: Harvest after frost kills top growth. Store tubers in cool dry place. Replant the crown buds.

name: Youngberry
description: Dewberry × loganberry hybrid. Sweet dark berries, good for eating fresh. Popular in SA and VIC.
notes: Prune like blackberry. Thornless variety available.

name: Yuzu
description: Japanese citrus prized for its intensely fragrant zest — a floral, tart blend of lemon, mandarin and grapefruit. The zest is used in sauces, dressings, desserts and the iconic yuzu kosho.
notes: Cold hardier than most citrus — tolerates light frost. Fruits are seedy and tart so rarely eaten fresh. The zest and juice are the prize. Thornless varieties preferred. Harvest winter.

name: Zinnia
description: Brilliantly coloured annual that attracts butterflies and bees. Available in almost every colour from white to near-black.
notes: Water at soil level to prevent powdery mildew. Excellent cut flower — the more you cut, the more they bloom.

name: Zucchini
description: Extremely productive. One or two plants usually sufficient for a family.
notes: Hand pollinate if few bees. Harvest at 15-20cm for best flavour. Powdery mildew common in humid weather. Remove powdery mildew leaves to prevent spread.

name: Chestnut
description: Large, long-lived deciduous tree producing sweet, starchy nuts. One of the few truly sweet, low-fat nuts. Chinese and Japanese varieties are more disease-resistant than European types.
notes: Plant at least two trees for cross-pollination. Harvest when burrs split and nuts fall naturally — do not pick early. Store in cool, humid conditions. Prefers slightly acidic soil.
plantingGuide: Plant bare-root or potted trees in winter to early spring. Dig a hole twice the width of the root ball. Stake young trees for the first two years. Mulch heavily to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Avoid waterlogged soils.

name: Walnut
description: Majestic long-lived tree prized for rich, oily nuts. English (Persian) walnut is most commonly grown for eating. Produces juglone, a compound that suppresses many nearby plants.
notes: Keep away from vegetable gardens — juglone in roots and leaves inhibits many plants including tomatoes and potatoes. Harvest when green husks start to blacken and split. Cure nuts in a cool, airy spot for 2–3 weeks.
plantingGuide: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant. Choose a large open site well away from vegetable beds. Needs deep, well-drained fertile soil. Water deeply but infrequently once established. Minimal pruning needed — remove crossing branches in late summer only.

name: Hazel
description: Multi-stemmed shrub or small tree producing clusters of hazelnuts. Extremely versatile — can be grown as a hedge, coppiced for poles, or trained as a small tree. Catkins provide early pollen for bees.
notes: Plant two or more different varieties for reliable nut set. Harvest in late summer/autumn when nuts start to fall. Can be coppiced every 7–10 years to rejuvenate and harvest poles.
plantingGuide: Plant bare-root shrubs in winter. Tolerates partial shade but nuts better in full sun. Suits most well-drained soils including clay. Mulch around base. Remove suckers from the base unless growing as a multi-stem clump.

name: Macadamia
description: Australia's own commercial nut tree, native to SE Queensland and NE NSW rainforests. Rich, buttery kernels with the hardest shell of any nut. A beautiful ornamental tree as well as productive.
notes: Do NOT use phosphorus fertiliser — proteaceous plants are extremely sensitive to phosphate and can die. Harvest by collecting fallen nuts. Crack shells with a specialised cracker or vice. Nuts are ripe when husks turn brown and split.
plantingGuide: Plant grafted trees for earlier fruiting and known variety performance. Needs well-drained, slightly acidic soil rich in organic matter. Water regularly in dry periods, especially in first 3 years. Avoid frost. Mulch well but keep mulch away from trunk.

name: Pecan
description: Very large deciduous tree native to North America, producing rich, buttery nuts. Needs a long hot growing season to ripen nuts. One of the largest trees in the home orchard — plan for the long term.
notes: Needs two different varieties for cross-pollination (type 1 and type 2 flowering). Zinc deficiency is common — supplement with zinc sulphate if leaves show yellowing. Harvest when husks split in autumn.
plantingGuide: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil with plenty of room. Pecans have deep taproots — do not plant in shallow soil over rock. Water deeply in dry summer periods. Top-dress with compost annually. Very patient — nuts improve in quality as the tree matures.

name: Pistachio
description: Drought-hardy, long-lived tree suited to hot, dry summers and cool winters. One male tree pollinates up to 8 female trees. Thrives in conditions that would stress other fruit trees — poor soil, heat, and low rainfall.
notes: Requires both male and female trees — plant one male per 5–8 females. Needs hot summers (above 38°C) to ripen nuts fully. Harvest when hulls turn pink-red and slip off easily. Do not irrigate in the month before harvest.
plantingGuide: Plant in extremely well-drained, even sandy or rocky soil. Pistachios hate waterlogging more than almost any other tree. Established trees need very little water or fertiliser. Avoid high-nitrogen feeds. Patience required — production builds slowly over 10+ years.
pollination: Separate male and female trees required
